clean_story:
Stellar furnaces. Alchemy without magic.

The phrase nucleosynthesis—how stars cook metals—comes from nucleus (atom’s core) and synthesis (to build). Stars don’t "make" metals like a blacksmith; they fuse lighter atoms into heavier ones, then scatter them when they die.

A star’s core is a pressure cooker. Hydrogen atoms smash together, forming helium—like squeezing two drops of water into one bigger drop, but with energy released as light. In massive stars, helium fuses into carbon, oxygen, even iron. Each step requires more heat, like a stove turning from simmer to sear. Iron is the dead end: fusing it absorbs energy, so the star’s core collapses. Boom. A supernova explodes, flinging gold, silver, and uranium into space at 15,000 miles per second—faster than a bullet.

Your blood’s iron? Forged in a star that died before the Sun was born. Earth’s gold? Likely from neutron stars crashing together, spraying heavy metals like a cosmic piñata.

Three related subjects are neutron star collisions, planetary metal deposits, and human uses for stardust.
